sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 54-year-old male, presents with a complaint of stabbing, shooting pain in the right cheekbone area of his face.
He reports that the pain has been ongoing for approximately two to three months and is exacerbated by certain temperatures and cold weather.
He describes the pain as severe, a 10 out of 10, and states that it is like someone is stabbing him with an ice pick.
He denies numbness or tingling in the affected area and reports that his smile and vision are unaffected.
He has tried ibuprofen, Motrin, and tiger balm with little relief.
He has a past medical history of occasional headaches, but no history of migraines or brain tumors.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any other symptoms in other organs or systems.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of occasional headaches, but no history of migraines or brain tumors.
He has not mentioned any other significant medical conditions.
Current medications:
The patient is currently taking Tylenol for occasional headaches.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
The patient's facial symmetry appears normal.
He is able to follow my finger in both directions.
He reports numbness on the right side of his face when I run cotton tape along the side of his face.
He reports that the left side of his face feels sharp when I poke it with a needle, while the right side feels dull.
Test Results:
The patient's MRI shows a small blood vessel abutting and perhaps pinching the trigeminal nerve.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has a condition called trigeminal neuralgia, where the compression of the trigeminal nerve causes shooting electrical pain in the face.
Problem list: Trigeminal neuralgia; active
Plan:
I will prescribe Tegretol for the patient to try and reduce the excitability of the nerve.
I will also recommend genetic testing to ensure the medication is safe for him to take.
If the medication does not provide adequate pain relief, we will discuss surgical options to decompress the nerve.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient in one month to check on the effectiveness of the medication and discuss further treatment options if necessary.
